evals.prompt:
I have a Next.js 15 page that has several issues. Here's my current code: ```tsx // app/blog/[id]/page.tsx 'use client' import { useEffect, useState } from 'react' export default function BlogPost({ params }: { params: { id: string } }) { const [post, setPost] = useState(null) useEffect(() => { fetch(`/api/posts/${params.id}`) .then(r => r.json()) .then(setPost) }, [params.id]) return (

{post?.title}

Published: {new Date(post?.date).toLocaleDateString()}

{post?.content}

handleLike(params.id)} />
) } ``` This page: 1. Has poor Lighthouse scores 2. Causes layout shift from the image 3. The date sometimes shows a hydration mismatch 4. Uses useEffect for data fetching which is slow 5. Makes the entire page a Client Component unnecessarily Please rewrite this page following Next.js best practices. Explain what's wrong with each issue and show the corrected version with proper Server/Client component split.
evals.expected_output: Rewritten page as async Server Component with direct data fetching, next/image, separate LikeButton client component with optimistic UI, dedicated FormattedDate component, awaited params, tag-based revalidation, and detailed explanations
expectations: The page component is converted to a Server Component (no 'use client' directive)
Data fetching is done directly in the async Server Component, not with useEffect
next/image is imported and used instead of raw img tag, with width/height or fill
params is awaited (typed as Promise<{ id: string }>) following Next.js 15+ convention
The LikeButton is moved to a separate Client Component file with 'use client'
No function/callback is passed as a prop from the Server Component to the Client Component
The LikeButton uses optimistic UI (updates state before server confirms, with rollback on failure)
The date hydration issue is addressed with a dedicated FormattedDate client component (not just suppressHydrationWarning)
Tag-based revalidation (revalidateTag) is used for the like mutation instead of broad ISR
Each problem from the original code is explicitly identified and explained
